emotional_core: The quiet dread of realizing your analysis was confidently wrong — and the relief of finally understanding exactly why.
visual_metaphor: A calculator displaying a confident, plausible-looking wrong number — no alarm, no red light, no error — just a screen full of certainty about something false.
key_quotes: Your analysis will give you a wrong answer someday. Not an error. Not a crash. A perfectly confident wrong number.
Python didn't add 28 + 35 + 42. It concatenated them: '28' + '35' + '42' = '283542'.
Every silent bug in data analysis traces back to the same root: the programmer didn't understand what kind of thing they were holding.

story_frames.visual: Laptop screen in a calm, ordinary desk environment showing a Python terminal with sum() returning '283542' — highlighted in amber. No error message anywhere in view.
Close-up of a dark terminal: type('28') returning <class 'str'> — one line of code, clean monospace font, high contrast.
Side-by-side: a list [28, 35, 42] on the left and a dict {'name': 'Tarun', 'age': 28} on the right. Minimal styling, no decoration.
story_frames.text: Lists are rows. Dicts are records.

story_frames.visual: A 5-line for loop on the left visually collapsing into a single list comprehension on the right — split view, clean dark-mode editor.
Three notebook tabs open side by side, each with the same 12-line block highlighted in yellow — then all three replaced by a single def calculate_average(): function.
A minimal data pipeline diagram: Raw CSV → Type Check → Transform → Aggregate → Output. Simple labeled boxes, no decoration, clean sans-serif.
Terminal showing the corrected output — the same column summing to 105, not '283542'. Green text on dark background. Clean, quiet, resolved.
